title: Basic clothing repairs for non-sewers
subtitle: Replacing buttons, mending small rips, and darning thin patches without a sewing machine
excerpt: Extending the life of a garment by 9 months reduces its environmental footprint by 20 to 30 percent according to WRAP estimates. Most of the repairs that would otherwise send a garment to the bin take 5 to 20 minutes by hand. This guide covers the four most common repairs: button reattachment, seam split, small rip, and thin fabric patch.
sourceNotes: WRAP clothing longevity research (2017): Valuing Our Clothes. TRAID (Textile Reuse and International Development) garment repair guides. Worn Again charity repair workshop materials.

glossaryTerms.definition: An economic model that prioritising keeping products and materials in use for as long as possible through reuse, repair, and sharing, rather than a linear take-make-dispose pattern.

content.text: WRAP research found that 11 million garments are thrown away in the UK every week, of which a significant proportion are discarded because of minor damage: a missing button, a split seam, a small hole. The four repairs in this guide address the most common faults. A basic sewing kit (needle, thread in three or four colours, small scissors) costs 5 pounds and lasts years.
Match the new button to the remaining buttons in size, colour, and number of holes.
Thread a needle with a 40 cm length of thread. Double it and tie a knot at the end.

Push the needle through the fabric from the inside at the button position. Place the button over the exit point. Pass the needle up through one hole of the button and back down through the other (for a 2-hole button). For a 4-hole button, sew through two pairs of holes in a cross pattern.

Make 6 to 8 passes through the holes, then wind the thread 4 times around the thread shank between the button and the fabric to form a reinforcing collar. This prevents the button from pulling flat against the fabric when the garment is done up.
Push the needle through to the inside of the fabric and make two small stitches through the existing threads to lock. Trim.

A split seam (where the stitching has come undone but the fabric is intact) is the fastest repair. Turn the garment inside out. Align the seam edges and pin them if helpful. Use a running stitch: push the needle in and out of the fabric at 3 to 4 mm intervals along the seam line, overlapping by 10 mm with the existing stitching at both ends of the split. Pull the thread taut but not so tight it gathers the fabric. Tie off at each end.
Closing a small rip or hole

For a straight rip in a woven fabric (not a tear across the weave): press the edges of the rip together from the inside. Use a running stitch parallel to the rip line, 3 to 4 mm from the edge on each side, closing the gap. For a curved or irregular hole up to 10 mm across: use a darning stitch. Run parallel stitches across the hole on the inside (the stitch passes over the hole from one side to the other at 2 mm intervals). Then run a second set of parallel stitches perpendicular to the first, weaving over and under the first set. This creates a woven fabric replacement inside the damaged area.

Where a garment is thinning at the elbow, knee, or crotch seam but not yet holed, a fusible iron-on interfacing patch on the inside prevents the fabric from tearing. Cut the interfacing slightly larger than the thinning area. Place it adhesive side down on the inside of the garment. Press with a hot iron (no steam) for 10 seconds. Allow to cool before handling. The interfacing reinforces the thin area from inside without being visible on the outside. This is part of the
in practice: extending use before the garment fails rather than after.

attrs.symptom: Thread breaks when pulling through fabric

content.text: Use a shorter length of thread (30 to 40 cm maximum) and use thread designed for hand-sewing rather than machine thread, which is wound tighter and breaks more easily when used with a hand needle.

attrs.symptom: Darned area is visible on the outside of the garment

content.text: Match the thread colour more closely. A contrasting-colour darn is a visible mend; if the goal is invisible repair, invest in a thread in a very close match to the garment fabric.
